FT_IC1.evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes data from the ABCD Study; baseline N=12,000, ages 9-10 years, across 4 waves of longitudinal data

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis reported; parallel process latent growth curve models examining coupling of growth trajectories across early adolescence

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed empirical article in Journal of Research on Adolescence

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Social media use measured via Youth Screen Time Survey, summing time on texting, social networking sites, and video chatting. Paired with cognitive performance outcomes (crystallized abilities, inhibitory control/attention, processing speed) which are individual-trait-level. Parallel process models report associations between SM use trajectories and cognitive performance trajectories

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric results reported throughout: growth trajectory slopes, intercept-slope covariances (e.g., for social media slope β=0.36, p<.001), correlations between composite variables, and indirect effects in parallel process models

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with quantitative results; parallel process latent growth curve models with significance tests on cross-construct associations; results table shows parameter estimates and p-values for all tested associations

FT_EC7_reason: Design is longitudinal (4 waves across years). Social media use functions as DV in the parallel growth curve models (trajectories of SM use are the outcome being modeled). No analysis identified where SM is IV (predicting another variable); SM appears only as DV throughout. All three conditions met: longitudinal design, SM-as-DV-only, no SM-as-IV analysis
notes: EXCLUDE code FT-EC7. Paper meets FT-IC1 through FT-IC4 but fails the EC7 exclusion criterion for longitudinal SM-as-DV-only designs

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; N=8,699 ABCD participants, ages 10 at baseline; ABCD-specific estimates reported separately in Table 2 alongside Generation R cohort

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; target trial emulation (TTE) framework with G-formula estimating hypothetical intervention effects on modifiable factors and internalizing symptoms

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed empirical article in Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1 finds generic "screen time (hr/day)" variable. Step 2 composite check: Methods describe screen time as one of multiple modifiable factors (ST, sleep, PA, social connections) but do not specify which screen activities comprise the composite. No breakdown provided of TV, gaming, social media, or other modalities. Multi-device aggregated time without per-device isolation. Paper does not isolate phone/SM from other screen modalities. Other side is internalizing symptoms (depression/anxiety), which is individual-trait, but phone/SM exposure fails isolation requirement

notes: EXCLUDE code FT-EC2. Fails FT-IC2 because screen time composite is not demonstrated to be exclusively phone/SM items; multi-device aggregated time without separate phone/SM breakout

FT_IC1.evidence: Data from Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study; 11,819 youth participants, baseline ages 9.92 years (SD=0.62); four waves of data from ABCD 5.0 release

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; random-intercepts cross-lagged panel models (RI-CLPMs) examining longitudinal bidirectional associations; multiple-group analyses for moderation by OSA content type and child sex

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed empirical article in European Child & Adolescent Psychiatry

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Online social activity (OSA) time is a phone/SM-specific variable. Methods state OSA measured via Youth Screen Time Survey, summing "texting," "visiting social networking sites," and "video chatting." This composite is exclusively phone/SM/digital-socializing items. Paired with ADHD problems (individual-trait-level outcome assessing inattention, hyperactivity, impulsivity via CBCL). Results reported across all waves in RI-CLPM with numeric estimates. Meets both Step 1 and Step 2 criteria

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric results reported throughout: cross-lagged coefficients (e.g., OSA time T2→T3 to ADHD β=0.273***, p<.001), standardized path coefficients in Figure 1, Table 3 showing sex-stratified cross-lagged parameter estimates with 95% CIs, correlations in Table 2

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with quantitative results; RI-CLPM reports cross-lagged effects with significance testing; multiple-group analyses by sex with Satorra-Bentler chi-square tests; standardized coefficients and p-values for all associations

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply. Although design is longitudinal, the paper analyzes OSA time as both IV (predicting ADHD at later waves) and DV (predicted by ADHD at earlier waves) in cross-lagged panel structure. The presence of bidirectional/cross-lagged analysis (SM as both IV and DV in same model) disqualifies FT-EC7. Additionally, sex-moderated analyses reveal reverse effects (ADHD→OSA relationship differs by sex), confirming bidirectional structure
notes: INCLUDE. Meets FT-IC1, FT-IC5, FT-IC6, FT-IC2, FT-IC3, FT-IC4. FT-EC7 does not apply due to bidirectional cross-lagged design
